question.text: Which AIF category typically exhibits the deepest J Curve effect?

answer.text: Category I venture capital funds investing in early-stage start-ups
All categories exhibit identical J Curves

explanation.text: **The Logic:** VC funds (Cat I) exhibit the deepest J Curve because: (1) investments take the longest to mature (5-10 years), (2) early-stage companies have the highest mortality rate, (3) fees are charged from day one while value creation takes years, and (4) exits are most uncertain. Debt funds have shallow J Curves; hedge funds may not exhibit J Curves at all. **The Trap:** Category III hedge funds often invest in listed markets with frequent rebalancing, so they don't exhibit the classic J Curve pattern. The J Curve is primarily a PE/VC phenomenon. **Cross-Exam Context:** Comparing J Curve profiles across AIF categories is tested in XIX-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When presenting VC funds to investors, warn about the deep and long J Curve. A VC fund might show -20% to -30% returns in years 1-3 before turning positive. This is normal but psychologically challenging for investors not accustomed to alternative investments.
